title: Rust vs Go
description: Side-by-side comparison for Go developers learning Rust

comparisons.go:
x := 5
var x int = 5
comparisons.rust:
let x = 5;
let x: i32 = 5;
comparisons.note: Rust variables are immutable by default. Use `let mut` for mutable.

comparisons.go:
x := 5
x = 10 // All variables are mutable
comparisons.rust:
let mut x = 5;
x = 10; // Must declare with `mut`
comparisons.note: Rust requires explicit opt-in to mutability, preventing accidental changes.
Rust functions return the last expression without `return`. The return type follows `->`.
Rust uses snake_case for fields. Fields are private by default (use `pub` to export).
Rust methods are defined in `impl` blocks. `&self` is like a pointer receiver in Go.
Go interfaces are implicit; Rust traits must be explicitly implemented with `impl Trait for Type`.
Rust uses Result<T, E> instead of multiple returns. The `?` operator propagates errors concisely.
Rust threads are OS threads. For async concurrency similar to goroutines, use tokio or async-std.

comparisons.go:
ch := make(chan int)
go func() { ch <- 42 }()
val := <-ch
comparisons.note: Rust's mpsc channels are multi-producer, single-consumer. Use crossbeam for more channel types.

comparisons.go:
s := []int{1, 2, 3}
s = append(s, 4)
comparisons.rust:
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
v.push(4);
comparisons.note: Rust's Vec<T> is like Go slices. Rust also has slices (&[T]) which are borrowed views into contiguous data.
Rust's HashMap returns Option instead of a zero value. No built-in map literal syntax.

comparisons.go:
s := "hello"
bs := []byte(s)
rs := []rune(s)
comparisons.note: Rust has two string types: String (owned, heap) and &str (borrowed slice). Both are UTF-8.

comparisons.go:
x := 42
p := &x
fmt.Println(*p)
comparisons.note: Rust references are always valid (no null). Mutable refs (&mut T) require exclusive access.

comparisons.go:
package main
import "fmt"
comparisons.rust:
mod my_module;
use std::fmt;
comparisons.note: Rust modules map to files. Visibility is private by default; use `pub` to export.
Rust tests live in the same file with #[test]. Run with `cargo test`.
Rust enums are algebraic data types — each variant can hold different data (like tagged unions).
Rust generics are monomorphized at compile time (zero runtime cost). Use trait bounds for constraints.
Rust closures capture variables by reference or by move. The compiler infers the capture mode.

comparisons.go:
f, _ := os.Open("file.txt")
defer f.Close()
comparisons.rust:
let f = File::open("file.txt")?;
// f is automatically closed when it goes out of scope
comparisons.note: Rust uses RAII (Drop trait) instead of defer. Resources are cleaned up automatically when the owner goes out of scope.
Rust has no null/nil. Option<T> forces you to handle the absence case at compile time.
Rust match is exhaustive — the compiler ensures you handle all variants. No runtime type checking needed.

comparisons.go:
var mu sync.Mutex
mu.Lock()
// critical section
mu.Unlock()
comparisons.note: Rust's Mutex wraps the data it protects, making it impossible to access data without locking. The lock is released automatically via RAII.
